elements.sent-2411-001: Variant calling is a fundamental task in genomic research, essential for detecting genetic variations such as single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and insertions or deletions (indels).
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper presents an enhancement to DeepChem\cite{Ramsundar-et-al-2019 , a widely used open source drug discovery framework, through the integration of DeepVariant\cite{poplin2018universal .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, we introduce a variant calling pipeline that leverages DeepVariant’s convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to improve the accuracy and reliability of variant detection.
elements.sent-2411-004: The implemented pipeline includes stages for realignment of sequencing reads, candidate variant detection, and pileup image generation, followed by variant classification using a modified Inception v3\cite{szegedy2015rethinkinginceptionarchitecturecomputer model.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Our work adds a modular and extensible variant calling framework to the DeepChem framework and enables future work integrating DeepChem's drug discovery infrastructure more tightly with bioinformatics pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-006: Variant calling identifies genetic variants such as single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and small insertions or deletions (indels) from high-throughput sequencing data.
elements.sent-2411-007: These genetic variants are foundational for studies in population genetics, disease etiology, and precision medicine, where they inform risk prediction models and therapeutic interventions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Standard approaches to variant calling, such as those employed by GATK \cite{mckenna2010genome and SAMtools \cite{li2009sequence , rely on probabilistic models that face limitations in processing ambiguous or low-quality data.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Such methods struggle with noisy genomic regions, leading to reduced sensitivity and specificity, especially in regions of low coverage or with complex structural variations.
elements.sent-2411-010: DeepVariant\cite{poplin2018universal , developed by Google, uses a CNN to reframe the variant calling task as an image classification problem.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Pileup images are generated from sequencing reads and analyzed by the CNN to distinguish true variants from sequencing errors.
elements.sent-2411-012: This approach outperforms traditional heuristic-based methods, achieving higher accuracy in variant detection, particularly across diverse sequencing platforms and in regions where conventional tools exhibit reduced performance.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, while the code for DeepVariant is accessible on platforms such as GitHub, the code is written in C++ and is challenging to modify or extend.
elements.sent-2411-014: The architecture and components are fixed within the provided framework, making it difficult for researchers to adapt DeepVariant to explore novel hypotheses or improve specific sub-components for their experimental needs.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address the need for modular open-source implementations of computational genomic tools, we integrate an implementation of DeepVariant into the DeepChem\cite{Ramsundar-et-al-2019 framework.}
elements.sent-2411-016: DeepChem, an open-source Python library designed for scientific machine learning and deep learning, has established itself as a versatile platform for applications in molecular machine learning ranging from the MoleculeNet benchmark suite\cite{wu2018moleculenet to protein-ligand interaction modeling \cite{gomes2017atomic , and generative modeling of molecules \cite{frey2022fastflows , among others.}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: [H] \includegraphics[width=130 pt]{img1.png} \caption{The figure on the top depicts the DeepVariant genetic variant calling pipeline, which inputs a reference genome and sequencing reads, identifies candidate variants, encodes pileup images, and processes them through a CNN based on the Inception v3 architecture to produce genotypic likelihoods, leading to variant calls. The figure on the bottom shows the original Inception v3 architecture as used in DeepVariant, including its stem layer, Inception and reduction layers, global average pooling layer, and final output.} \label{fig:dv_pipeline_inceptionv3}
elements.sent-2411-017: Deepchem's modular architecture, comprising components such as data loaders, featurizers, splitters, models, and metrics, provides an extensible system that supports the development of custom workflows.
elements.sent-2411-018: The incorporation of DeepVariant into DeepChem significantly broadens its functionality, enabling variant calling workflows to be conducted entirely within an open-source Python ecosystem.
elements.sent-2411-019: We anticipate this infrastructure will enable subsequent computational work exploring the intersection of drug discovery and bioinformatics.
elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \includegraphics[width=213pt]{img2.png} \caption{A schematic of DeepVariant pipeline in DeepChem, that includes realigning reads, detecting candidate variants, generating pileup images, and generating output VCF(Variant Call Format) after passing the images through a trained CNN} \label{fig:deepvariant_pipeline_in_deepchem}
elements.sent-2411-020: The integration of DeepVariant into the DeepChem framework is centered around three primary components: realignment and candidate variant detection, pileup image generation, and a deep learning model designed for variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-021: These components are implemented through modular featurizers and a custom convolutional neural network (CNN).. \cite{poplin2017practical.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The first stage of the variant calling pipeline involves realigning the input sequencing reads and identifying candidate variants.
elements.sent-2411-023: Reads are provided in BAM\cite{li2009sequence format, which stores compressed alignments of sequencing reads to a reference genome.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The realignment process introduces haplotype awareness, crucial for improving variant detection accuracy in regions of genetic variation.
elements.sent-2411-025: To achieve this, smaller windows are created around regions exhibiting evidence of variation, which are flanked by sequences that match the reference genome.
elements.sent-2411-026: Within these windows, a de-Bruijn\cite{pevzner2001eulerian graph is constructed to represent potential haplotypes.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Nodes in the graph represent k-mers from the aligned reads, and edges indicate possible transitions between k-mers.
elements.sent-2411-028: Edges with insufficient support are pruned to eliminate low-confidence haplotypes.
elements.sent-2411-029: The remaining paths through the graph represent potential haplotypes, which are then used to realign the original reads.
elements.sent-2411-030: The Smith-Waterman algorithm is employed to realign reads to the most likely haplotype, followed by realignment of the haplotype to the reference genome.
elements.sent-2411-031: This step generates candidate variants by identifying deviations from the reference sequence.
elements.sent-2411-032: This stage is implemented via the RealignerFeaturizer, which processes aligned BAM files, constructs the assembly graph, performs realignment, and outputs a set of candidate variants.
elements.sent-2411-033: This featurizer is optimized for scalability and efficiency using the pysam\cite{Gilman_Janzou_Guittet_Freeman_DiOrio_Blair_Boyd_Neises_Wagner_2019 library, which facilitates BAM file manipulation and processing.}
elements.sent-2411-034: An optimized Smith-Waterman (SW) alignment algorithm is used for realignment, implemented in Python using PyTorch.
elements.sent-2411-035: It leverages GPU-accelerated tensor operations to perform sequence alignment with improved computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-036: Unlike traditional implementations such as the SIMD-optimized SSW Library, which are written in C/C++ and focus on low-level hardware optimizations, this method exploits Python's high-level interface for rapid prototyping while maintaining competitive performance through PyTorch's underlying CUDA backend.
elements.sent-2411-037: Alignment scores were computed using vectorized operations, with substitution scores derived from binary match/mismatch masks.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=213pt]{img3.png} \caption{A schematic of the RealignerFeaturizer in DeepChem, to generate pileup information from input files, process them to get Allele Counts, select candidate regions based on them, process candidate regions by building de-Bruijn Graphs, traversing them to generate candidate haplotypes and realigning them using the Striped Smith Waterman Algorithm \cite{Zhao2013}} \label{fig:realigner_featurizer}
elements.sent-2411-038: Once candidate variants have been identified, the next stage involves generating pileup images, a core feature of DeepVariant’s variant calling process.
elements.sent-2411-039: A pileup image represents the aligned sequencing reads at a particular genomic position, with each row corresponding to a read and each column to a base in the read sequence or reference genome.
elements.sent-2411-040: The image provides a two-dimensional matrix summarizing the sequence alignment, which is used as input to the deep learning model.
elements.sent-2411-041: The PileupImageFeaturizer is responsible for creating these images, with six channels encoding different features of the sequencing data.
elements.sent-2411-042: Channel 0 encodes base intensities, while Channel 1 captures base quality information.
elements.sent-2411-043: Channel 2 encodes mapping quality, and Channel 3 represents the strand orientation (i.e., whether the read is from the forward or reverse strand).
elements.sent-2411-044: Channel 4 indicates whether the read supports a variant, and Channel 5 encodes the difference between the read and the reference sequence.
elements.sent-2411-045: These multi-channel images provide a rich representation of the underlying sequencing data, enhancing the ability of the deep learning model to distinguish between true variants and sequencing artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-046: The deep learning model employed in this workflow is a custom CNN, derived from the Inception v3 architecture, which is specifically tailored for genomic variant calling from pileup images.
elements.sent-2411-047: The Inception v3 model's convolutional layers are modified to handle the six-channel input format.
elements.sent-2411-048: It outputs a probability score for each candidate variant, indicating whether it is a true variant or a sequencing error.
elements.sent-2411-049: The model integrates into DeepChem’s model library, allowing users to easily swap models if desired or integrate variant calling into larger machine learning workflows, such as multi-task learning frameworks or pipelines incorporating other types of genomic data.
elements.sent-2411-050: The integration of DeepVariant into DeepChem marks a progression in the development of open-source tools for genomic variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-051: By embedding advanced deep learning techniques within a flexible machine learning framework, this integration improves accessibility and customizability of variant calling infrastructure for a wide range of research applications.
elements.sent-2411-052: DeepChem's modular architecture enables easy adaptation, allowing researchers to explore new methodologies, such as neural network-based sequencing, within genomic data analysis workflows.
elements.sent-2411-053: Genetic variant calling plays a pivotal role in drug discovery by enabling the identification of genetic mutations, that influence disease mechanisms and individual responses to therapeutic interventions.
elements.sent-2411-054: These genetic variants provide key insights into target identification and validation, as well as patient stratification, which are essential in the development of personalized medicine.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the context of drug discovery, variant calling can characterize genetic mutations associated with drug resistance or sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-056: Variant data can be leveraged to predict adverse drug reactions or optimize drug dosages, reducing the risk of toxicity and enhancing treatment outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-057: Genetic variants can also induce conformational changes in proteins, altering their function or interaction with small molecules, thus influencing drug efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-058: Understanding the structural impact of variants enables researchers to design drugs that specifically target altered protein conformations or restore normal function, facilitating the development of structure-based drug design strategies.
elements.sent-2411-059: Genetic variant calling can consequently serve as a foundation for linking genotypic information to phenotypic consequences, with structural biology providing a framework for explaining how these variants affect molecular interactions, drug binding, and protein function, ultimately helping to accelerate the drug discovery process.
elements.sent-2411-060: The original implementation of DeepVariant was written in a combination of C++ and Python.
elements.sent-2411-061: This hybrid design provided efficiency but also introduced complexity, requiring proficiency in both languages for development and maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-062: In contrast, the integration of DeepVariant into the DeepChem framework has been implemented entirely in Python.
elements.sent-2411-063: This offers several advantages, including simplified code management, easier extensibility, and a lower barrier to entry for developers and researchers.
elements.sent-2411-064: Python's widespread use in the scientific computing community, coupled with its robust ecosystem of libraries for machine learning and bioinformatics, ensures that this implementation remains both accessible and flexible.
elements.sent-2411-065: The all-Python approach facilitates rapid prototyping and adaptation to new experimental requirements, which is particularly beneficial for ongoing research and iterative development in genomic variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-066: DeepVariant integrated into DeepChem consists of approximately 1,500 lines of code, written entirely in Python, whereas the original DeepVariant codebase contains around 35,000 lines of code, including its core implementation in C++ and Python, along with scripts, utilities, and tests.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Comparison of lines of code between DeepVariant and DeepVariant (DeepChem)\label{tab:loc_comparison}} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}l p{3 cm} r@{}} Method Language(s) Lines of Code (approx.) DeepVariant C++/Python ~35,000 DeepVariant (DeepChem) Python ~1,500 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-067: An evaluation of performance metrics (Table 2) indicates some discrepancies between the original DeepVariant implementation and its integration into DeepChem.
elements.sent-2411-068: The integration was trained on the high-coverage, whole-genome synthetic diploid sample CHM1-CHM13, with code implemented in PyTorch\cite{paszke2019pytorch and utilizing an 80-20 data split on Google Colab\cite{googlecolab .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: For instance, while DeepVariant's performance on indel detection remains strong (F1 = 0.958), the version integrated into DeepChem shows a slightly lower F1 score of 0.882.
elements.sent-2411-070: This reduction may stem from variations in the CNN backbone or subtle differences in how pileup images are processed.
elements.sent-2411-071: For SNP detection, a similar trend is observed, with the DeepChem version yielding lower precision and recall compared to the original.
elements.sent-2411-072: Another area requiring attention is computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-073: While the incorporation of deep learning methods has enhanced the accuracy of variant detection, the computational cost remains a bottleneck, particularly when processing large-scale genomic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-074: The current pipeline could benefit from further optimization, such as the parallelization of realignment and pileup image creation processes, as well as the introduction of more efficient deep learning models designed specifically for high-throughput genomic analysis.
elements.sent-2411-075: Addressing these challenges will be critical for improving the scalability of the pipeline in large-scale studies or clinical settings.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Comparison of variant calling methods for indels and SNPs in diploid sample CHM1-CHM13. The fully Python-based modular implementation of DeepVariant within DeepChem demonstrates comparable performance to widely used variant calling tools such as GATK, Samtools, and FreeBayes, in terms of precision, recall, and F1 score, for both indels and SNP detection.\label{tab:variant_calling_comparison}} \begin{tabular*}{\columnwidth}{@{\extracolsep\fill}lcccc@{\extracolsep\fill}} Method Type F1 Precision Recall Deep Variant (DeepChem) Indel 0.88243 0.81101 0.95451 Deep Variant Indel 0.95806 0.92866 0.98868 GATK Indel 0.93268 0.89504 0.97363 Samtools Indel 0.88976 0.79089 0.96611 FreeBayes Indel 0.90430 0.83025 0.99305 Deep Variant (DeepChem) SNP 0.88243 0.81191 0.95451 Deep Variant SNP 0.99100 0.98888 0.99319 GATK SNP 0.96646 0.95685 0.97627 Samtools SNP 0.96818 0.94380 0.99378 FreeBayes SNP 0.96910 0.94837 0.99075 \end{tabular*}
elements.sent-2411-076: Despite these limitations, our modular open-source Python implementation allows users to easily swap individual components, such as the CNN architecture or realignment algorithm, as new methods and technologies emerge.
elements.sent-2411-077: The integration of DeepVariant\cite{poplin2018universal to DeepChem enables researchers to utilize advanced deep learning methods for genomics within a customizable and modular Python framework, expanding the scope for applying machine learning techniques in genomics.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The approach presented, which combines CNN-based variant detection with pileup image generation, simplifies the variant calling process and enhances accessibility for diverse research applications.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, further refinement is required to achieve the levels of accuracy reported by the original DeepVariant model.
elements.sent-2411-080: Computational efficiency also remains a critical challenge, particularly in large-scale studies, where the current framework may not yet provide the required scalability.
elements.sent-2411-081: Genetic variant calling is crucial in drug discovery by identifying mutations that influence disease mechanisms and therapeutic responses, aiding in target identification, patient stratification, and optimizing treatment outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-082: It also links genetic variants to structural changes in proteins, facilitating structure-based drug design and advancing the drug discovery process.
elements.sent-2411-083: As an open-source tool, this implementation encourages ongoing community-driven improvements.
elements.sent-2411-084: By offering a platform that supports the incorporation of novel methodologies, this integration holds potential for future advancements in personalized medicine, population genetics, and other areas of genomic research.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Variant calling is a fundamental task in genomic research for detecting genetic variations such as single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and insertions or deletions (indels).
elements.sent-2411-086: This paper presents an enhancement to DeepChem \cite{Ramsundar-et-al-2019 , a widely used open source drug discovery framework, through the integration of DeepVariant \cite{poplin2018universal .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: We introduce DeepChem-Variant, a variant calling pipeline that leverages DeepVariant's convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to improve variant detection accuracy and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-088: DeepChem-Variant has stages for realignment of sequencing reads, candidate variant detection, and pileup image generation, followed by variant classification using either the original modified Inception V3 model or our novel MobileNetV2 implementation.
elements.sent-2411-089: We performed 3 case studies to validate our approach.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our work also contributes optimized utility functions for genomic data formats, including enhanced DataLoaders for BAM, SAM, and CRAM files, and an optimized FASTALoader.
elements.sent-2411-091: These implementations collectively provide a modular and extensible variant calling framework within DeepChem, enabling tighter integration of DeepChem's drug discovery infrastructure with bioinformatics pipelines for future research.
elements.sent-2411-092: Variant calling identifies single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and insertions/deletions (indels) from sequencing data, foundational for population genetics, disease etiology, and precision medicine applications including risk prediction and therapeutic interventions.
elements.sent-2411-093: Standard approaches like GATK \cite{mckenna2010genome and SAMtools \cite{li2009sequence use probabilistic models that struggle with ambiguous or low-quality data.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: These methods face challenges in noisy genomic regions, reducing sensitivity and specificity in low-coverage areas or regions with complex structural variations.
elements.sent-2411-095: DeepVariant \cite{poplin2018universal, poplin2017practical , developed by Google, uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) \cite{krizhevsky2012imagenet to reframe the variant calling task as an image classification problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Pileup images (Section~\ref{sec:2.2 ) are generated from sequencing reads and analyzed by the CNN to distinguish true variants from sequencing errors.}
elements.sent-2411-097: However, while the code for DeepVariant is accessible on platforms such as GitHub, part of the code is written in C++ and is challenging to modify or extend.
elements.sent-2411-098: To address the need for modular open-source implementations of computational genomic tools, we integrate an implementation of DeepVariant as DeepChem-Variant into the DeepChem \cite{Ramsundar-et-al-2019 framework.}
elements.sent-2411-099: DeepChem, an open-source Python library designed for scientific machine learning and deep learning, has established itself as a versatile platform for applications in molecular machine learning ranging from the MoleculeNet benchmark suite \cite{wu2018moleculenet to protein-ligand interaction modeling \cite{gomes2017atomic , and generative modeling of molecules \cite{frey2022fastflows , among others.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The DeepChem community of developers and contributors actively maintains all implementations in this system, which are validated through continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-101: The incorporation of DeepChem-Variant into DeepChem significantly broadens its functionality, enabling variant calling workflows to be conducted entirely within an open-source Python ecosystem.
elements.figure-2411-006: [H] \includegraphics[width=200 pt]{d1.png} \caption{DeepVariant workflow: Reference genome and aligned reads generate candidate variants, which are converted to pileup images, processed by a trained CNN to produce genotype likelihoods, and finally output as variant calls.} \label{fig:dv_pipeline_inceptionv3}
elements.figure-2411-007: [H] \includegraphics[width=160 pt]{deepchemvariant.png} \caption{DeepChem-Variant workflow: Reference genome (FASTA) and aligned reads (BAM) feed into the DeepVariant class containing candidate variant featurizer, pileup featurizer, trained CNN (MobileNetV2 or InceptionV3), and VCF converter to produce output Variant Call Format (VCF) files.} \label{fig:deepvariant_pipeline_in_deepchem}
elements.sent-2411-102: DeepChem-Variant has three primary components: candidate variant detection, pileup image generation, and a deep learning model designed for variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-103: These components are implemented through modular featurizers and a custom convolutional neural network (CNN).
elements.sent-2411-104: To efficiently handle various sequence alignment formats, we developed specialized utility classes including BAMLoader, SAMLoader, and CRAMLoader, enabling seamless integration with diverse genomic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-105: We also enhanced the FASTALoader for efficient reference genome access and significantly optimized the FASTAFeaturizer to process raw nucleotide sequences directly, rather than converting to one-hot encoded representations, resulting in a 210-fold acceleration in processing speed.
elements.sent-2411-106: The first stage involves realigning input sequencing reads and identifying candidate variants through the CandidateFeaturizer class.
elements.sent-2411-107: Reads are provided in BAM\cite{li2009sequence format, storing compressed alignments of sequencing reads to a reference genome.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The featurizer supports optional realignment for improved variant detection accuracy, optional multiprocessing (achieving 8× speedup), and optional labeling for training purposes when VCF ground truth is provided.
elements.sent-2411-109: The realignment process introduces haplotype awareness through realignment using the realign\_read method, using Striped Smith Waterman algorithm \cite{Zhao2013 to improve read positioning.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The count\_alleles method then tallies base occurrences at each position from aligned reads, accounting for CIGAR operations (which encode how sequencing reads align to the reference genome, indicating matches, insertions, deletions, and other alignment operations) to handle insertions, deletions, and matches.
elements.sent-2411-111: The detect\_candidates method identifies potential variants by comparing observed bases against the reference, flagging positions where alternative alleles exceed the minimum count and frequency thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-112: Finally, the left\_align\_indel method standardizes indel representations by shifting them to their leftmost valid positions, ensuring consistent variant notation across samples.
elements.sent-2411-113: An optimized Smith-Waterman alignment algorithm performs realignment using PyTorch's GPU-accelerated tensor operations.
elements.sent-2411-114: Unlike traditional optimized implementations in C/C++, this approach exploits Python's high-level interface while maintaining competitive performance through PyTorch's CUDA backend.
elements.sent-2411-115: Alignment scores are computed using vectorized operations with substitution scores derived from binary match/mismatch masks.
elements.sent-2411-116: Left-alignment of indels proves critical for variant standardization.
elements.sent-2411-117: Without left-alignment, candidate detection yielded inconsistent results across samples (average: 342,536 variants), while left-alignment produced 1,727,087 average standardized candidates, demonstrating the importance of variant normalization.
elements.sent-2411-118: The min\_count parameter, which is the minimum number of reads that must support an alternate allele for it to be considered a candidate variant, significantly impacts candidate sensitivity and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-119: It helps filter out sequencing errors and reduces false positives by requiring multiple independent observations of the same change.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Impact of min\_count parameter on candidate detection using HG004 (GIAB consortium data, using Novaseq whole exome sequencing with IDT capture at 100x coverage)\label{tab:min_count_impact}} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}c c p{4cm}@{}} min\_count Total Candidates Computational Impact 1 8,420,830 High downstream execution time 2 (default) 1,770,108 Optimal balance \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-120: The default min\_count=2 provides optimal balance, as reducing to 1 substantially increases downstream processing without meaningful recall improvement.
elements.sent-2411-121: This stage is implemented using the pysam \cite{Gilman_Janzou_Guittet_Freeman_DiOrio_Blair_Boyd_Neises_Wagner_2019 library for efficient BAM and FASTA file manipulation.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=180 pt]{d3.png} \caption{CandidateFeaturizer workflow: Input data flows through realignment of reads, allele counting at genomic positions, variant detection based on frequency thresholds, and left-alignment of indels to produce standardized candidate variants as output.} \label{fig:realigner_featurizer}
elements.sent-2411-122: Once candidate variants have been identified, the next stage involves generating pileup images, a core feature of DeepVariant.
elements.sent-2411-123: A pileup image represents aligned sequencing reads at a genomic region centered on a candidate variant position.
elements.sent-2411-124: Each row corresponds to a read, and each column represents a genomic coordinate within the pileup window.
elements.sent-2411-125: The PileupFeaturizer is responsible for creating these images, with six channels encoding different features of the sequencing data.
elements.sent-2411-126: The deep learning model employed in this workflow is a custom CNN, derived from either the \cite{szegedy2015rethinkinginceptionarchitecturecomputer architecture or MobileNetV2 \cite{sandler2018mobilenetv2 , which is specifically tailored for genomic variant calling from pileup images.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We integrated both Inception V3 and MobileNetV2 architectures into DeepChem's core model library.
elements.sent-2411-128: The Inception V3 model's convolutional layers are modified to handle the six-channel input format, while our MobileNetV2 implementation leverages its efficient inverted residual structure and linear bottlenecks for improved computational efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-129: Both models output a probability score for each candidate variant, indicating whether it is a true variant or a sequencing error.
elements.sent-2411-130: The models integrate into DeepChem's model library, allowing users to easily swap between Inception V3 and MobileNetV2 implementations if desired or integrate variant calling into larger machine learning workflows, such as multi-task learning frameworks or pipelines incorporating other types of genomic data.
elements.figure-2411-010: [H] \includegraphics[width=200 pt]{D4.png} \caption{Architectures of Inception V3 and MobileNetV2 as used in DeepVariant.} \label{fig:dv_inceptionv3}
elements.sent-2411-131: We validated DeepChem-Variant across three genomic contexts using DeepVariant as baseline, since no established ground truth exists for these specialized datasets.
elements.sent-2411-132: DeepChem-Variant values in Table~\ref{tab:combined_comparison represent the subset of DeepVariant calls that our method successfully detected.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Sensitivity measures DeepChem-Variant's ability to recover DeepVariant's calls, calculated as the percentage of DeepVariant variants successfully identified by DeepChem-Variant.
elements.sent-2411-134: VCF outputs from both methods were compared by intersecting variant positions and alleles between them.
elements.sent-2411-135: CRISPR Off-Target Detection: Synthetic datasets simulated CRISPR-Cas9 \cite{jinek2012programmable off-target effects at PAM sites (protospacer adjacent motifs) with insertion, deletion, and random edit patterns across NNNNGATT and NGG motifs.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Off-target detection is critical for CRISPR therapeutic safety, as unintended edits can cause harmful mutations.
elements.sent-2411-137: Ancient DNA Analysis: Simulated characteristic ancient DNA damage patterns including C→T transitions, fragmentation, and age-dependent preservation effects spanning 100 to 50,000 years.
elements.sent-2411-138: Ancient DNA analysis enables evolutionary studies and population history reconstruction but requires specialized variant calling due to extreme degradation.
elements.sent-2411-139: Population Genomics: Analyzed whole exome sequencing from adult female, adult male, and pediatric male samples to assess demographic-specific variant detection.
elements.sent-2411-140: Population genomics applications require consistent variant calling across diverse samples for disease association studies and personalized medicine.
elements.figure-2411-011: [H] \caption{Variant detection sensitivity across genomic contexts\label{tab:combined_comparison}} \columnwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{@{}l l c c c@{}} Context Sample/Type DeepVariant DeepChem-Variant Sensitivity (\%) \multirow{6}{*}{CRISPR} NNNNGATT Insertion 2103 1717 81.6 NNNNGATT Deletion 2284 1819 79.6 NNNNGATT Random 2051 1623 79.1 NGG Insertion 284 251 88.4 NGG Deletion 312 282 90.4 NGG Random 298 274 91.9 \multirow{3}{*}{Ancient DNA} Recent (100y) 32,847 30,942 94.2 Medieval (800y) 51,293 47,251 92.1 Neanderthal (50,000y) 78,164 68,503 87.6 \multirow{3}{*}{Population} Adult Female 61,245 55,732 91.0 Adult Male 59,874 54,605 91.2 Pediatric Male 60,298 54,992 91.2 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-141: DeepChem-Variant marks a progression in the development of open-source tools for genomic variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-142: DeepChem's modular architecture enables easy adaptation, allowing researchers to explore new methodologies, within genomic data analysis workflows.
elements.sent-2411-143: The original DeepVariant combined C++ and Python, creating complexity requiring proficiency in both languages.
elements.sent-2411-144: DeepChem-Variant's all-Python implementation (approximately 1,500 lines versus DeepVariant's 35,000 as mentioned in Table~\ref{tab:loc_comparison ) simplifies development, reduces barriers to entry, and leverages Python's scientific computing ecosystem for easier extensibility and rapid prototyping.}
elements.sent-2411-145: DeepChem-Variant offers two CNN architectures: Inception V3 and MobileNetV2 Our models were trained on HG001, HG002, HG004, and HG005 WES deduplicated samples at 100x coverage from IDT NovaSeq (300 million rows), compared to production DeepVariant models trained on 8-9 fold larger multi-technology GIAB datasets (2.6 billion rows) \cite{covering_all_bases and all models were validated on HG003.}
elements.sent-2411-146: The code was implemented in PyTorch \cite{paszke2019pytorch on Google Colab \cite{googlecolab .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: More details about hyperparameters and system specifications are mentioned in Appendix \ref{app:expinfo , details about datasets are mentioned in Appendix \ref{app:datasets .}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \caption{Comparison of lines of code between DeepVariant and DeepVariant (DeepChem)\label{tab:loc_comparison}} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}l p{3 cm} r@{}} Method Language(s) Lines of Code (approx.) DeepVariant C++/Python ~35,000 DeepChem-Variant Python ~1,500 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-148: An evaluation of performance metrics (Table~\ref{tab:performance_metrics ) indicates some discrepancies between the original DeepVariant implementation and DeepChem-Variant.}
elements.sent-2411-149: This is due to limited training data diversity in our experiments compared to production DeepVariant (discussed in section \ref{sec:disc:perf_eff ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: The MobileNetV2 (3.4 million parameters) results are particularly notable given its significantly fewer parameters compared to InceptionV3 (24 million parameters) and lower ImageNet accuracy, yet achieving competitive performance in genomic variant calling.
elements.sent-2411-151: Despite these limitations, our modular open-source Python implementation allows users to easily swap components, such as the CNN architecture or realignment algorithm, as new methods and technologies emerge.
elements.figure-2411-013: \caption{Performance comparison of variant calling methods\label{tab:performance_metrics}} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}l l c c c@{}} Method Variant Type Recall Precision F1-Score DeepVariant INDEL 0.971 0.993 0.982 DeepVariant SNP 0.988 0.998 0.993 DeepChem-Variant (MobileNetV2) INDEL 0.912 0.934 0.923 DeepChem-Variant (MobileNetV2) SNP 0.922 0.941 0.943 DeepChem-Variant (InceptionV3) INDEL 0.923 0.951 0.933 DeepChem-Variant (InceptionV3) SNP 0.931 0.954 0.939 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-152: In this work, we introduce DeepChem-Variant which enables researchers to utilize advanced deep learning methods for genomics within a customizable Python framework, expanding machine learning applications in genomics.
elements.sent-2411-153: While performance differences compared to the original CNN implementation were observed, due to training on smaller datasets and architectural choices, the integration within DeepChem facilitates rapid future improvements.
elements.sent-2411-154: This will also allow for the easier incorporation of novel genomic analysis methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-155: We note that the observed performance reflects the current training data and model architecture, and leave further optimization for future work.
elements.sent-2411-156: As an open-source tool, we anticipate community contributions will drive further enhancements, ultimately benefiting areas such as personalized medicine and population genetics.
elements.sent-2411-157: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning.
elements.sent-2411-158: There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.. \bibliographystyle{icml2025 \onecolumn.}
elements.sent-2411-159: This project added significant functionality to DeepChem through new classes and enhancements spanning featurizers, data loaders, models, and variant calling infrastructure.
elements.figure-2411-014: [H] \caption{Code contributions to DeepChem framework\label{tab:code_contributions}} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}l l p{10cm}@{}} Class Name Parent Class Description SAMFeaturizer Featurizer Processes SAM alignment files BAMFeaturizer Featurizer Processes BAM alignment files CRAMFeaturizer Featurizer Processes CRAM alignment files FASTAFeaturizer Featurizer Enhanced sequence processing with 210× speedup SAMLoader DataLoader Loads SAM format files BAMLoader DataLoader Loads BAM format files CRAMLoader DataLoader Loads CRAM format files FASTALoader DataLoader Loads reference genome sequences MobileNetV2Model TorchModel Efficient CNN for variant classification InceptionV3Model TorchModel High-accuracy CNN for variant classification CandidateFeaturizer Featurizer Identifies potential variant sites PileupFeaturizer Featurizer Generates multi-channel alignment images DeepChemVariant TorchModel Complete variant calling pipeline \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-161: DeepChem-Variant (both InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) utilized a pileup image representation with a window size of 221 base pairs, capturing 100 read depths across 6 channels encoding base identity, base quality, mapping quality, strand orientation, variant support, and reference match indicators.
elements.sent-2411-162: Training was conducted using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-3, batch size of 128, and 10 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-163: The model was trained on Google Colab's L4 GPU infrastructure with 8 data loader workers for parallel data processing.
elements.sent-2411-164: Variant candidates were filtered using minimum allele count and frequency thresholds of 2 reads and 1\% respectively, ensuring adequate support for downstream classification while maintaining computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-165: DeepChem-Variant (both InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) was trained on a constrained dataset comprising HG001, HG002, HG004, and HG005 WES deduplicated samples at 100x coverage from a single sequencing platform (IDT NovaSeq, 12 GB total).
elements.sent-2411-166: In contrast, production DeepVariant models utilize extensive multi-technology GIAB datasets that are ~8-9 fold larger, incorporating data from diverse sequencing platforms including HiSeqX, NovaSeq, and PCR-positive samples across multiple WES capture kits, different sequencing depths, and various sample preparation methods \cite{covering_all_bases .}
elements.sent-2411-167: This substantial difference in training data scale, technological diversity, and sample heterogeneity results in reduced performance due to our model's limited exposure to the full spectrum of sequencing artifacts and variant patterns present in real-world genomic data.
elements.sent-2411-168: The CandidateVariantFeaturizer algorithm processes genomic data in sliding windows to identify potential variant sites.
elements.sent-2411-169: For each genomic region, it extracts aligned reads and reference sequences, optionally performs Smith-Waterman realignment to improve accuracy, then counts allele frequencies at each position.
elements.sent-2411-170: Candidate variants are detected by comparing observed alleles against the reference using minimum count and frequency thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-171: Finally, indel variants are left-aligned to ensure standardized representation.
elements.sent-2411-172: The algorithm returns an array of candidate variants with associated metadata for downstream analysis.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{CandidateVariantFeaturizer} \label{alg:candidatefeaturizer} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: BAM file $B$, FASTA file $F$ \STATE Output: Candidate variant array \FOR{each region $(chrom, start, end)$} \STATE $reads \gets fetch(B, chrom, start, end)$ /* extract aligned reads */ \STATE $ref\_seq \gets fetch(F, chrom, start, end)$ /* extract reference sequence */ \IF{realign enabled} \STATE $reads \gets smith\_waterman\_realign(reads, ref\_seq)$ /* improve alignment accuracy */ \ENDIF \STATE $counts \gets count\_alleles(reads, ref\_seq)$ /* tally base frequencies */ \STATE $variants \gets detect\_candidates(counts, \text{min\_count}, \text{min\_frac})$ /* identify variant sites */ \FOR{each variant $v \in variants$} \STATE $v \gets left\_align\_indel(v)$ /* standardize representation */ \STATE output.append(v) /* add to result set */ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE Return: candidate variants with metadata \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Smith-Waterman Alignment} \label{alg:smithwaterman} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Query sequence $Q$, reference sequence $R$ \STATE Output: Aligned query sequence \STATE $H, E, F \gets zeros(|Q|+1, |R|+1)$ /* alignment, gap matrices */ \STATE $pointer \gets zeros(|Q|+1, |R|+1)$ /* traceback directions */ \STATE $max\_score, max\_pos \gets 0, (0,0)$ /* track optimal alignment */ \FOR{$i = 1$ to $|Q|$} \FOR{$j = 1$ to $|R|$} \STATE $match \gets H[i-1,j-1] + score(Q[i], R[j])$ /* diagonal score */ \STATE $E[i,j] \gets \max(H[i-1,j] + gap\_open, E[i-1,j] + gap\_extend)$ /* vertical gap */ \STATE $F[i,j] \gets \max(H[i,j-1] + gap\_open, F[i,j-1] + gap\_extend)$ /* horizontal gap */ \STATE $H[i,j] \gets \max(0, match, E[i,j], F[i,j])$ /* local alignment score */ \IF{$H[i,j] > max\_score$} \STATE $max\_score, max\_pos \gets H[i,j], (i,j)$ /* update maximum */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE $aligned \gets traceback(pointer, max\_pos, Q)$ /* reconstruct alignment */ \STATE Return: $aligned$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-173: The Smith-Waterman algorithm performs optimal local sequence alignment using dynamic programming.
elements.sent-2411-174: It initializes three scoring matrices: H for alignment scores, E and F for gap penalties.
elements.sent-2411-175: The algorithm fills these matrices by calculating match/mismatch scores and gap costs, maintaining traceback pointers for reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-176: It identifies the maximum local alignment score during matrix filling, then traces back from this position to reconstruct the optimal alignment path.
elements.sent-2411-177: This implementation uses PyTorch tensors for vectorized operations, providing GPU acceleration while maintaining the algorithm's quadratic time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-178: The count\_alleles funtion tallies base frequencies at each genomic position by processing aligned reads.
elements.sent-2411-179: It initializes a dictionary array to store counts per position, then iterates through each read's CIGAR string to handle matches, insertions, and deletions appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-180: For matched regions, it extracts base calls and increments corresponding position counters.
elements.sent-2411-181: The algorithm advances position pointers based on CIGAR operations, ensuring proper coordinate mapping between read sequences and reference positions.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Count Alleles} \label{alg:countalleles} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Reads $R$, reference sequence $ref$, region start $start$ \STATE Output: Allele counts per position \STATE $counts \gets empty\_dict\_array(|ref|)$ /* one dict per position */ \FOR{each read $r \in R$} \IF{$r$ is unmapped or duplicate} \STATE continue /* skip low-quality reads */ \ENDIF \STATE $ref\_pos, query\_pos \gets r.start, 0$ /* initialize positions */ \FOR{each $(operation, length)$ in $r.cigar$} \IF{$operation == MATCH$} \FOR{$i = 0$ to $length-1$} \STATE $pos \gets ref\_pos + i - start$ /* convert to region coordinates */ \IF{$0 \leq pos < |ref|$ and $query\_pos + i < |r.sequence|$} \STATE $base \gets r.sequence[query\_pos + i]$ /* extract base call */ \STATE $counts[pos][base] \gets counts[pos][base] + 1$ /* increment count */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $ref\_pos, query\_pos \gets ref\_pos + length, query\_pos + length$ /* advance both */ \ELSIF{$operation == INSERTION$} \STATE $query\_pos \gets query\_pos + length$ /* advance query only */ \ELSIF{$operation == DELETION$} \STATE $ref\_pos \gets ref\_pos + length$ /* advance reference only */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $counts$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-182: The detect\_candidates function identifies potential variant sites by applying frequency-based filtering to allele counts.
elements.sent-2411-183: It examines each genomic position with coverage, calculates total read depth, and compares observed alleles against the reference.
elements.sent-2411-184: Variants are flagged as candidates if they differ from the reference allele and exceed both minimum count and frequency thresholds, helping reduce false positives from sequencing errors while retaining true biological variants.
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Detect Candidate Variants} \label{alg:detectcandidates} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Allele counts $counts$, reference $ref$, thresholds $min\_count$, $min\_frac$ \STATE Output: Candidate variant list \STATE $candidates \gets empty\_list()$ /* initialize result list */ \FOR{$i = 0$ to $|counts|-1$} \STATE $total \gets \sum(counts[i].values())$ /* sum all allele counts */ \IF{$total == 0$} \STATE continue /* skip positions with no coverage */ \ENDIF \STATE $ref\_base \gets ref[i]$ /* get reference allele */ \FOR{each $(base, count)$ in $counts[i]$} \IF{$base \neq ref\_base$ and $count \geq min\_count$ and $\frac{count}{total} \geq min\_frac$} \STATE $candidate \gets (i, ref\_base, base, count, total)$ /* create variant record */ \STATE $candidates.append(candidate)$ /* add to candidates */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $candidates$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-185: The left align indels algorithm standardizes indel representation by shifting variants to their leftmost valid position.
elements.sent-2411-186: It first checks if the variant is a SNP (equal lengths or different first bases), returning unchanged if so.
elements.sent-2411-187: For indels, it trims common prefix and suffix sequences, then iteratively shifts the variant leftward by comparing flanking bases from the reference genome until no further movement is possible, ensuring consistent variant notation across different calling methods.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Left Align Indels} \label{alg:leftalign} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Chromosome $chrom$, position $pos$, reference allele $ref$, alternate allele $alt$, FASTA $fasta$ \STATE Output: Left-aligned position and alleles \IF{$|ref| == |alt|$ or $ref[0] \neq alt[0]$} \STATE Return: $(pos, ref, alt)$ /* SNP, no alignment needed */ \ENDIF \STATE $seq, seq\_alt, left \gets ref, alt, pos$ /* initialize working variables */ \WHILE{$|seq| > 1$ and $|seq\_alt| > 1$ and $seq[-1] == seq\_alt[-1]$} \STATE $seq, seq\_alt \gets seq[:-1], seq\_alt[:-1]$ /* trim common suffix */ \ENDWHILE \WHILE{$|seq| > 1$ and $|seq\_alt| > 1$ and $seq[0] == seq\_alt[0]$} \STATE $seq, seq\_alt \gets seq[1:], seq\_alt[1:]$ /* trim common prefix */ \STATE $left \gets left + 1$ /* adjust position */ \ENDWHILE \WHILE{$left > 1$} \STATE $prev\_base \gets fasta.fetch(chrom, left-2, left-1)$ /* get preceding base */ \IF{deletion and $seq[-1] == prev\_base$} \STATE $seq \gets prev\_base + seq[:-1]$ /* shift deletion left */ \STATE $left \gets left - 1$ /* update position */ \ELSIF{insertion and $seq\_alt[-1] == prev\_base$} \STATE $seq\_alt \gets prev\_base + seq\_alt[:-1]$ /* shift insertion left */ \STATE $left \gets left - 1$ /* update position */ \ELSE \STATE break /* cannot shift further */ \ENDIF \ENDWHILE \STATE Return: $(left, seq, seq\_alt)$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-188: The PileupFeaturizer algorithm converts genomic variants into multi-channel images for CNN processing.
elements.sent-2411-189: It creates a 6-channel tensor where each channel captures different alignment properties: base identity (A/C/G/T encoded as intensity values), base quality scores, mapping quality, read strand direction, and matches to alternate/reference alleles.
elements.sent-2411-190: The algorithm centers a fixed-width window around each variant position, extracts aligned reads from the BAM file, sorts them by quality, and populates the image tensor row-by-row.
elements.sent-2411-191: The reference sequence occupies the bottom row with maximum quality values, while aligned reads fill remaining rows based on their CIGAR alignment coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-192: This transforms raw sequencing data into structured image format suitable for deep learning variant classification.
elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{PileupFeaturizer} \label{alg:pileupfeaturizer} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: BAM file $B$, FASTA file $F$, candidates $C$ \STATE Output: Multi-channel pileup images dataset \STATE $n \gets |C|$ /* number of candidate variants */ \STATE $X \gets zeros(n, channels, height, window)$ /* image tensor */ \STATE $y \gets zeros(n)$ if labeled else None /* labels if training */ \FOR{$i = 0$ to $n-1$} \STATE $chrom, pos, ref, alt \gets C[i][0:4]$ /* extract variant info */ \STATE $start \gets pos - window/2$ /* define window boundaries */ \STATE $end \gets pos + window/2 + 1$ \STATE $ref\_seq \gets fetch\_reference(F, chrom, start, end)$ /* get reference */ \STATE $reads \gets fetch\_reads(B, chrom, start, end)$ /* get aligned reads */ \STATE $reads \gets sort(reads, \text{by mapping quality})$ /* prioritize high-quality reads */ \STATE $pile \gets zeros(channels, height, window)$ /* initialize image */ \FOR{$col = 0$ to $window-1$} \STATE $pile[0, height-1, col] \gets base\_to\_intensity(ref\_seq[col])$ /* reference base */ \STATE $pile[1:5, height-1, col] \gets [1.0, 1.0, 1.0, alt\_match]$ /* reference row */ \ENDFOR \FOR{$row = 0$ to $height-2$} \STATE $read \gets reads[row]$ /* process each read */ \FOR{each aligned position $(qpos, rpos)$ in $read$} \IF{$start \leq rpos < end$} \STATE $col \gets rpos - start$ /* column in image */ \STATE $base \gets read.sequence[qpos]$ /* read base */ \STATE $pile[0, row, col] \gets base\_to\_intensity(base)$ /* base identity */ \STATE $pile[1, row, col] \gets read.quality[qpos] / 40.0$ /* base quality */ \STATE $pile[2, row, col] \gets read.mapping\_quality / 60.0$ /* mapping quality */ \STATE $pile[3, row, col] \gets 0.0$ if reverse else $1.0$ /* strand */ \STATE $pile[4, row, col] \gets 1.0$ if $base == alt$ else $0.0$ /* alt match */ \STATE $pile[5, row, col] \gets 1.0$ if $base == ref$ else $0.0$ /* ref match */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE $X[i] \gets pile$ /* store completed image */ \IF{labeled} \STATE $y[i] \gets C[i][-1]$ /* extract label if training */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $NumpyDataset(X, y)$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-193: The DeepChem-Variant algorithm implements a complete variant calling pipeline using deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-194: It first extracts candidate variants from aligned reads using frequency thresholds, then generates multi-channel pileup images around each candidate site.
elements.sent-2411-195: These images are processed through a convolutional neural network (MobileNetV2) in batches to predict genotype probabilities (reference, heterozygous, or homozygous alternate).
elements.sent-2411-196: The algorithm computes genotype quality scores from prediction confidence, filters out reference calls, and writes remaining variants to a standard VCF file with proper formatting and metadata.
elements.algorithm-2411-007: \caption{DeepChem-Variant} \label{alg:deepchemvariant} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: BAM file $B$, FASTA file $F$, output VCF path $O$ \STATE Output: VCF file with variant calls \STATE $candidates \gets CandidateFeaturizer(B, F)$ /* extract potential variants */ \IF{$candidates$ is empty} \STATE $write\_empty\_vcf(O, F)$ /* create empty VCF with header */ \STATE Return: $O$ \ENDIF \STATE $pileup\_images \gets PileupFeaturizer(B, F, candidates)$ /* generate 6-channel images */ \STATE $predictions \gets []$ /* initialize prediction array */ \FOR{$i = 0$ to $|pileup\_images|$ step $batch\_size$} \STATE $batch \gets pileup\_images[i:i+batch\_size]$ /* create batch */ \STATE $batch\_preds \gets MobileNetV2(batch)$ /* predict genotype probabilities */ \STATE $predictions.append(batch\_preds)$ /* collect predictions */ \ENDFOR \STATE $all\_predictions \gets concatenate(predictions)$ /* combine batches */ \STATE $genotypes \gets argmax(all\_predictions)$ /* most likely genotype */ \STATE $gq \gets compute\_quality(all\_predictions)$ /* genotype quality scores */ \STATE $write\_vcf\_header(O, F, sample\_name)$ /* write VCF header */ \FOR{each $(candidate_i, genotype_i, quality_i) \in zip(candidates, genotypes, gq)$} \IF{$genotype_i == 0$} \STATE continue /* skip reference calls */ \ENDIF \STATE $chrom, pos, ref, alt \gets candidate_i[0:4]$ /* extract variant info */ \STATE $gt\_string \gets$ "0/1" if $genotype_i == 1$ else "1/1" /* format genotype */ \STATE $write\_vcf\_record(O, chrom, pos, ref, alt, gt\_string, quality_i)$ /* write variant */ \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $O$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-197: The complete MobileNetV2 architecture implements efficient mobile computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-198: It begins with standard convolution for initial feature extraction, then processes through configurable inverted residual blocks that balance accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-199: Each block configuration [t, c, n, s] specifies expansion ratio, output channels, repetition count, and stride.
elements.sent-2411-200: Width multipliers enable scaling model capacity for different resource constraints.
elements.sent-2411-201: Final layers include high-dimensional feature mapping (1280 channels), global average pooling for spatial dimension reduction, and linear classification head.
elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{MobileNetV2 Network} \label{alg:mobilenetv2} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Image $I$ with $C_{in}$ channels, input size $H \times W$ \STATE Params: Width multiplier $\alpha$, class count $N_{\text{class}}$ \STATE $x \gets \text{ConvBNReLU}(I, \text{out\_channels}=32, \text{stride}=2)$ /* initial feature extraction */ \FOR{each block configuration $[t, c, n, s]$ in settings} \STATE $C_{out} \gets \lceil \frac{c \times \alpha}{8} \rceil \times 8$ /* apply width multiplier */ \FOR{$i = 1$ to $n$} \IF{$i = 1$} \STATE $x \gets \text{InvertedResidual}(x, C_{out}, \text{stride}=s, \text{expand\_ratio}=t)$ /* first block with stride */ \ELSE \STATE $x \gets \text{InvertedResidual}(x, C_{out}, \text{stride}=1, \text{expand\_ratio}=t)$ /* subsequent blocks */ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE $x \gets \text{ConvBNReLU}(x, \text{out\_channels}=1280)$ /* final feature mapping */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Mean}(x, \text{over spatial dims})$ /* global average pooling */ \STATE $y \gets \text{Linear}(x, \text{out\_features}=N_{\text{class}})$ /* classification layer */ \STATE Return: Class logits $y$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-202: The ConvBNReLU block is a standard convolutional building block optimized for mobile inference.
elements.sent-2411-203: The 3×3 convolution extracts spatial features while batch normalization stabilizes training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-204: ReLU6 activation (min(max(0, x), 6)) provides bounded non-linearity that improves quantization precision for mobile deployment, reducing numerical precision requirements compared to unbounded ReLU.
elements.algorithm-2411-009: \caption{ConvBNReLU Block} \label{alg:convbnrelu} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, input channels $C_{in}$, output channels $C_{out}$, stride $s$ \STATE $y \gets \text{Conv2D}(x, \text{kernel}=3 \times 3, \text{stride}=s, \text{padding}=1, \text{bias}=False)$ /* convolution */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize activations */ \STATE $y \gets \text{ReLU6}(y)$ /* bounded activation function */ \STATE Return: $y$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-205: The Inverted Residual block is a core MobileNetV2 innovation addressing traditional depthwise convolution limitations.
elements.sent-2411-206: The "inverted" design expands narrow input channels to higher dimensions (expansion phase), applies efficient depthwise convolution for spatial feature extraction (filtering phase), then compresses back to narrow output channels (projection phase).
elements.sent-2411-207: This pattern maintains information flow in high-dimensional space while keeping input/output narrow for efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-208: Linear bottlenecks (no activation after final projection) preserve information flow.
elements.sent-2411-209: Residual connections enable gradient flow and feature reuse when input/output dimensions align, following ResNet principles adapted for mobile efficiency.
elements.algorithm-2411-010: \caption{Inverted Residual Block} \label{alg:invertedresidual} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, input channels $C_{in}$, output channels $C_{out}$, stride $s$, expand ratio $t$ \STATE $C_{hidden} \gets C_{in} \times t$ /* calculate expanded channels */ \STATE $\text{use\_residual} \gets (s = 1) \land (C_{in} = C_{out})$ /* residual only if dimensions match */ \IF{$t = 1$} \STATE $y \gets \text{DepthwiseConv}(x, \text{kernel}=3 \times 3, \text{stride}=s)$ /* no expansion needed */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize */ \STATE $y \gets \text{ReLU6}(y)$ /* activate */ \STATE $y \gets \text{PointwiseConv}(y, C_{out})$ /* project to output channels */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize projection */ \ELSE \STATE $y \gets \text{PointwiseConv}(x, C_{hidden})$ /* expand channels */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize expansion */ \STATE $y \gets \text{ReLU6}(y)$ /* activate expanded features */ \STATE $y \gets \text{DepthwiseConv}(y, \text{kernel}=3 \times 3, \text{stride}=s)$ /* spatial filtering */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize filtering */ \STATE $y \gets \text{ReLU6}(y)$ /* activate filtered features */ \STATE $y \gets \text{PointwiseConv}(y, C_{out})$ /* project to output */ \STATE $y \gets \text{BatchNorm}(y)$ /* normalize final projection */ \ENDIF \IF{\text{use\_residual}} \STATE $y \gets x + y$ /* add residual connection */ \ENDIF \STATE Return: $y$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-210: InceptionV3 architecture implements the network-in-network design philosophy, where convolutional filters of various sizes (1×1, 3×3, 5×5) are applied in parallel within each module to capture features at multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-211: The architecture systematically processes images through a hierarchical feature extraction pipeline that begins with stem convolutions for low-level feature extraction, progresses through InceptionA modules for multi-scale pattern recognition, utilizes reduction modules to compress spatial dimensions while expanding channel depth, employs InceptionC modules with factorized 7×7 convolutions for efficient mid-level feature processing, and concludes with InceptionE modules for high-level abstraction.
elements.sent-2411-212: The network incorporates factorized convolutions that break down larger convolutions into smaller, more efficient sequences (such as decomposing 3×3 into 1×3 and 3×1), significantly reducing computational complexity while maintaining representational power.
elements.sent-2411-213: Auxiliary classifiers are strategically placed to provide intermediate supervision during training, acting as regularizers that combat vanishing gradients in deep networks.
elements.sent-2411-214: Dimensionality reduction techniques are employed throughout to control computational complexity without sacrificing model expressiveness.
elements.algorithm-2411-011: \caption{InceptionV3} \label{alg:inceptionv3} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Image $x$ with $C_{in}$ channels, size $299 \times 299$ \STATE Output: Class logits and auxiliary logits (if training) \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d\_1a\_3x3}(x)$ /* initial stem convolution */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d\_2a\_3x3}(x)$ /* stem progression */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d\_2b\_3x3}(x)$ /* stem completion */ \STATE $x \gets \text{MaxPool2d}(x, 3, 2)$ /* spatial downsampling */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d\_3b\_1x1}(x)$ /* channel reduction */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d\_4a\_3x3}(x)$ /* feature extraction */ \STATE $x \gets \text{MaxPool2d}(x, 3, 2)$ /* spatial downsampling */ \FOR{$i = 5b$ to $5d$} \STATE $x \gets \text{InceptionA}(x)$ /* parallel multi-scale convolutions */ \ENDFOR \STATE $x \gets \text{InceptionB}(x)$ /* reduction with stride 2 */ \FOR{$i = 6b$ to $6e$} \STATE $x \gets \text{InceptionC}(x)$ /* factorized 7×7 convolutions */ \ENDFOR \IF{training and aux\_logits} \STATE $aux \gets \text{InceptionAux}(x)$ /* auxiliary classifier */ \ENDIF \STATE $x \gets \text{InceptionD}(x)$ /* reduction with stride 2 */ \FOR{$i = 7b$ to $7c$} \STATE $x \gets \text{InceptionE}(x)$ /* high-level feature extraction */ \ENDFOR \STATE $x \gets \text{AdaptiveAvgPool2d}(x, (1, 1))$ /* global pooling */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Flatten}(x)$ /* vectorize features */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Dropout}(x)$ /* regularization */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Linear}(x, num\_classes)$ /* classification head */ \STATE Return: $x$ (and $aux$ if training) \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-215: This fundamental building block combines three essential operations: convolution for spatial feature extraction, batch normalization for training stability and gradient flow optimization, and ReLU activation for introducing non-linearity while preserving gradient propagation.
elements.sent-2411-216: The bias-free convolution design leverages batch normalization's inherent bias handling, reducing parameter redundancy.
elements.algorithm-2411-012: \caption{BasicConv2d} \label{alg:basicconv2d} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, output channels $C_{out}$, kernel params \STATE $x \gets \text{Conv2d}(x, C_{out}, \text{bias=False})$ /* convolution without bias */ \STATE $x \gets \text{BatchNorm2d}(x)$ /* normalize activations */ \STATE $x \gets \text{ReLU}(x)$ /* non-linear activation */ \STATE Return: $x$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-217: This multi-scale feature extraction module implements parallel processing branches with different receptive fields to capture diverse spatial patterns.
elements.sent-2411-218: The 1×1 branch captures point-wise features and cross-channel correlations, the 5×5 branch (preceded by 1×1 reduction) captures medium-scale spatial patterns, the double 3×3 branch efficiently approximates larger receptive fields while reducing computational cost, and the pooling branch preserves existing feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-219: Feature concatenation combines these diverse representations into a unified output tensor.
elements.algorithm-2411-013: \caption{InceptionA Module} \label{alg:inceptiona} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, pool features count \STATE $branch_{1×1} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 64, 1×1)$ /* direct 1×1 path */ \STATE $branch_{5×5} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 48, 1×1)$ /* 5×5 reduction */ \STATE $branch_{5×5} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{5×5}, 64, 5×5)$ /* 5×5 convolution */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 64, 1×1)$ /* double 3×3 reduction */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{3×3}, 96, 3×3)$ /* first 3×3 */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{3×3}, 96, 3×3)$ /* second 3×3 */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{AvgPool2d}(x, 3, 1, 1)$ /* pooling path */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{pool}, pool\_features, 1×1)$ /* pool projection */ \STATE $output \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{1×1}, branch_{5×5}, branch_{3×3}, branch_{pool}])$ /* combine paths */ \STATE Return: $output$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-220: This architectural transition module reduces spatial resolution from 35×35 to 17×17 while expanding channel depth from 288 to 768.
elements.sent-2411-221: Multiple reduction paths maintain feature diversity during downsampling: direct 3×3 convolution with stride-2 for efficient reduction, double 3×3 path for complex pattern preservation, and max pooling for spatial downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-222: The increased channel count compensates for spatial information loss.
elements.algorithm-2411-014: \caption{InceptionB Module} \label{alg:inceptionb} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 384, 3×3, \text{stride=2})$ /* direct reduction */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 64, 1×1)$ /* double 3×3 path */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 96, 3×3)$ /* expand channels */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 96, 3×3, \text{stride=2})$ /* reduce spatial */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{MaxPool2d}(x, 3, 2)$ /* pooling reduction */ \STATE $output \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{3×3}, branch_{dbl}, branch_{pool}])$ /* combine reductions */ \STATE Return: $output$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-223: This computational optimization module factorizes expensive 7×7 convolutions into more efficient asymmetric sequences.
elements.sent-2411-224: The 1×7 followed by 7×1 factorization reduces parameters from 49 to 14 while maintaining equivalent receptive field coverage.
elements.sent-2411-225: The double factorization path provides additional feature diversity through repeated asymmetric convolutions, enabling complex pattern recognition with reduced computational overhead.
elements.algorithm-2411-015: \caption{InceptionC Module} \label{alg:inceptionc} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, 7×7 channel count \STATE $branch_{1×1} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 192, 1×1)$ /* direct path */ \STATE $branch_{7×7} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, channels_{7×7}, 1×1)$ /* 7×7 factorization */ \STATE $branch_{7×7} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{7×7}, channels_{7×7}, 1×7)$ /* factorize to 1×7 */ \STATE $branch_{7×7} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{7×7}, 192, 7×1)$ /* factorize to 7×1 */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, channels_{7×7}, 1×1)$ /* double 7×7 path */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, channels_{7×7}, 7×1)$ /* first factorization */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, channels_{7×7}, 1×7)$ /* second factorization */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, channels_{7×7}, 7×1)$ /* third factorization */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 192, 1×7)$ /* final factorization */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{AvgPool2d}(x, 3, 1, 1)$ /* pooling path */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{pool}, 192, 1×1)$ /* pool projection */ \STATE $output \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{1×1}, branch_{7×7}, branch_{dbl}, branch_{pool}])$ /* combine paths */ \STATE Return: $output$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-226: This second reduction stage transitions from 17×17 to 8×8 spatial resolution while expanding channels from 768 to 1280.
elements.sent-2411-227: The module combines direct 3×3 reduction for efficiency with complex 7×7×3 factorized paths that maintain information richness through sequential asymmetric convolutions followed by spatial reduction.
elements.sent-2411-228: This design preserves feature diversity while preparing for final high-level processing.
elements.algorithm-2411-016: \caption{InceptionD Module} \label{alg:inceptiond} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 192, 1×1)$ /* 3×3 reduction path */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{3×3}, 320, 3×3, \text{stride=2})$ /* spatial reduction */ \STATE $branch_{7×7×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 192, 1×1)$ /* 7×7×3 path */ \STATE $branch_{7×7×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{7×7×3}, 192, 1×7)$ /* factorize 1×7 */ \STATE $branch_{7×7×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{7×7×3}, 192, 7×1)$ /* factorize 7×1 */ \STATE $branch_{7×7×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{7×7×3}, 192, 3×3, \text{stride=2})$ /* final reduction */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{MaxPool2d}(x, 3, 2)$ /* pooling reduction */ \STATE $output \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{3×3}, branch_{7×7×3}, branch_{pool}])$ /* combine reductions */ \STATE Return: $output$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-229: The module splits 3×3 convolutions into separate 1×3 and 3×1 branches, effectively doubling feature diversity by capturing horizontal and vertical patterns independently.
elements.sent-2411-230: Complex double-branch paths maximize representational capacity through parallel processing of complementary feature patterns, providing rich feature representations for final classification decisions.
elements.algorithm-2411-017: \caption{InceptionE Module} \label{alg:inceptione} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$ \STATE $branch_{1×1} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 320, 1×1)$ /* direct path */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 384, 1×1)$ /* 3×3 split preparation */ \STATE $branch_{3×3a} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{3×3}, 384, 1×3)$ /* horizontal split */ \STATE $branch_{3×3b} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{3×3}, 384, 3×1)$ /* vertical split */ \STATE $branch_{3×3} \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{3×3a}, branch_{3×3b}])$ /* combine splits */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 448, 1×1)$ /* double 3×3 path */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 384, 3×3)$ /* expand features */ \STATE $branch_{dbla} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 384, 1×3)$ /* horizontal split */ \STATE $branch_{dblb} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{dbl}, 384, 3×1)$ /* vertical split */ \STATE $branch_{dbl} \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{dbla}, branch_{dblb}])$ /* combine splits */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{AvgPool2d}(x, 3, 1, 1)$ /* pooling path */ \STATE $branch_{pool} \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(branch_{pool}, 192, 1×1)$ /* pool projection */ \STATE $output \gets \text{Concatenate}([branch_{1×1}, branch_{3×3}, branch_{dbl}, branch_{pool}])$ /* combine all paths */ \STATE Return: $output$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-231: This auxiliary classifier module addresses vanishing gradient problems in deep networks by providing intermediate supervision during training.
elements.sent-2411-232: Positioned at the network's midpoint, it processes intermediate features through spatial reduction, channel manipulation, and classification layers.
elements.sent-2411-233: The auxiliary loss signal improves gradient flow to earlier network layers, enhancing training convergence and preventing gradient degradation.
elements.sent-2411-234: During inference, this module is bypassed to maintain computational efficiency.
elements.algorithm-2411-018: \caption{InceptionAux Module} \label{alg:inceptionaux} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Feature map $x$, number of classes \STATE $x \gets \text{AvgPool2d}(x, 5, 3)$ /* spatial reduction */ \STATE $x \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 128, 1×1)$ /* channel reduction */ \STATE $x \gets \text{BasicConv2d}(x, 768, 5×5)$ /* feature expansion */ \STATE $x \gets \text{AdaptiveAvgPool2d}(x, (1, 1))$ /* global pooling */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Flatten}(x)$ /* vectorize */ \STATE $x \gets \text{Linear}(x, num\_classes)$ /* classify */ \STATE Return: $x$ \end{algorithmic}
